source.journal_name: Romanian Journal of Information Science and Technology

paper.title: Numerical simulation of toxic chemical transport after accidental release at chemical plant
paper.abstract: CFD modeling for atmospheric pollution prediction in case of accidental solid propellant burning at chemical plant. Model based on transport equation and potential flow. Finite difference schemes of splitting used. Application to Pavlograd Chemical Plant for missile solid propellant accidents.

verdict.summary: CFD modeling paper with well-justified mathematical formalism. Transport equations (Eq. 1-2), chemical transformation (Eq. 3-8), potential flow (Eq. 9-10), and finite difference schemes (Eq. 11-17) are all necessary for the numerical simulation. Practical application to emergency response.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** CFD modeling for toxic gas dispersion after chemical plant accidents
**Level:** 3 (Medium)
**Rationale:** Develops novel quick-computing CFD model using finite difference schemes for practical emergency response. Not a simple application but not proving fundamental theorems either.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 18 (transport equation, chemical reactions, potential flow, finite difference schemes, risk formula)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~36 (18 equations x 2)
Expected range (Level 3): 10-40
Ratio: 0.9x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - equations are standard for CFD numerical simulation and essential for the methodology.

### Necessity Test
- Transport equation (Eq. 2): Necessary for pollutant dispersion modeling
- Chemical transformation (Eq. 3-8): Necessary for NO/NO2/O3 chemistry
- Potential flow model (Eq. 9-10): Necessary for air flow computation
- Finite difference schemes (Eq. 11-17): Necessary for numerical solution
- Risk formula (Eq. 18): Necessary for probabilistic risk assessment

**Verdict:** All formalism necessary and appropriate for numerical CFD paper.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

No self-citations detected. Reference list covers relevant prior work in CFD modeling and atmospheric dispersion.

---

## Structural Analysis

Clear methodology with governing equations, numerical scheme, and results. No explicit limitations section. Code developed (Air2D program) but not publicly available.

---

## Conclusion

Solid applied CFD paper with appropriate mathematical formalism for emergency response modeling. The formalism is essential for the numerical methods used, not decorative.

paper.title: Enhanced Cellular Automata with Autonomous Agents for Covid-19 Pandemic Modeling
paper.abstract: Model for epidemics evolution using enhanced cellular automata with autonomous agents. Simulations include COVID-19 specific details: atypicality, proximity for infection, long gestation time. Four improvements proposed: contextual CA, internal timer, FIFO memory for hospital, global control loop.

verdict.summary: Novel epidemic simulation model combining cellular automata with autonomous agents. Mathematical parameters (probability distributions, timer values, FIFO sizes) are necessary for the simulation framework. Four distinct enhancements proposed and validated through experiments.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Epidemic modeling using enhanced cellular automata with autonomous agents
**Level:** 2-3 (Low-Medium)
**Rationale:** Novel combination of CA + agents for epidemic simulation. Contributes four enhancements to basic model. Not proving theorems but proposing new modeling approach.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 formal numbered definitions
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: ~5 (probability parameters, simulation rules)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~10
Expected range (Level 2-3): 5-25
Ratio: 0.4-0.67x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - minimal formalism focused on simulation parameters.

### Necessity Test
- Probability parameters (pf, pl, pr, pb, ps): Necessary for agent movement rules
- Timer parameters (Ti, Th): Necessary for disease progression modeling
- FIFO size (H): Necessary for hospital capacity modeling
- Recovery rate (R): Necessary for outcome modeling

**Verdict:** All parameters necessary for simulation framework specification.

### Issues
None identified. The paper uses appropriate level of formalism for computational simulation.

---

## Citation Analysis

No excessive self-citation. References cover CA theory, epidemic modeling, and COVID-19 background.

---

## Structural Analysis

Well-structured with clear presentation of model enhancements. Multiple experiments with varying parameters. Implemented in Processing language but code not publicly available.

---

## Conclusion

Timely contribution during COVID-19 pandemic. The CA + agent model provides useful simulation framework. Formalism is minimal and appropriate for computational modeling paper.

paper.title: Connecting Research from Assistive Vision and Smart Eyewear Computing with Crisis Management and Mitigation Systems: A Position Paper
paper.abstract: Position paper examining transfer of scientific contributions from assistive vision research (Senses++ project) to crisis management systems. Three contributions: life abstraction for lifelogging, video broadcasting modalities, and assistive/mediated/augmented vision. Evaluated against six crisis management objectives.

verdict.summary: POSITION PAPER with deliberately limited empirical contribution. Presents ideas for transferring assistive vision research to crisis management. No unnecessary formalism - paper appropriately uses prose to describe concepts. Limited score reflects position paper nature, NOT formalism theater.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Position paper on transferring assistive vision research to crisis management
**Level:** 1-2 (Position paper - limited contribution by design)
**Rationale:** This is explicitly labeled as a POSITION PAPER. It presents ideas and opportunities for research transfer, not novel algorithms or proofs.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 0

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: 0
Expected range (Level 1-2): 0-15
Ratio: 0x (within range)

**Assessment:** Proportionate - position paper appropriately uses no mathematical formalism.

### Necessity Test
No formal elements to evaluate. The paper uses prose descriptions which is appropriate for a position paper discussing research directions and potential applications.

**IMPORTANT NOTE:** This is a position paper. The LIMITED classification reflects the inherent nature of position papers (ideas rather than implementations), NOT any formalism problems. The paper is honest about its contribution type.

### Issues
None identified. No formalism theater - the paper does not dress up simple concepts with unnecessary math.

---

## Citation Analysis

5 self-citations out of ~41 references (12%). All self-citations are to the Senses++ project which is the focus of the paper - highly relevant. No citation manipulation.

---

## Structural Analysis

Clear structure following position paper format. Table 1 summarizes contributions vs. crisis management objectives. No empirical validation (expected for position paper). References to prior Senses++ implementations.

---

## Conclusion

This is an honest position paper that:
1. Clearly labels itself as such in the title
2. Uses no unnecessary formalism
3. Presents reasonable ideas for research transfer

The LIMITED score reflects that position papers have limited empirical contribution by design. This is NOT a criticism - the paper serves its intended purpose. No formalism theater detected.

paper.title: The Social Networks information noise in crisis situations
paper.abstract: Research on information noise in social networks during crisis situations. Data collected from Twitter in January 2020. Analysis of tweets related to earthquake (Cuba/Jamaica 7.7M) and coronavirus. Shannon entropy and filtering rules used to identify information noise.

verdict.summary: Applied research on social media noise filtering during crises. Shannon entropy formula (Eq. 3) and probability calculations are standard information theory - necessary for quantifying information quality. Logical filter rules are practical contributions.

papers.provenance:

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Social network information noise analysis during crisis situations
**Level:** 2 (Low)
**Rationale:** Applies standard information theory (Shannon entropy) to Twitter data filtering. No novel algorithms proposed.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 formal definitions
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 3 (probability, entropy, source characteristics)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~6
Expected range (Level 2): 0-15
Ratio: 0.4x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - uses only standard information theory formulas.

### Necessity Test
- Probability formula (Eq. 1): Standard, necessary for probability calculation
- Shannon Entropy (Eq. 3): Standard information theory, necessary for noise analysis
- Source efficiency/redundancy: Standard metrics, necessary for evaluation

**Verdict:** All formalism is standard information theory, necessary for the methodology.

### Issues
None identified. The paper uses appropriate level of formalism for social media analysis.

---

## Citation Analysis

No excessive self-citation. References cover social media analysis, disaster response, and information theory.

---

## Structural Analysis

PHP code snippet included (Listing 1). Practical filtering rules (R1-R8) provided. Real-world data from Cuba/Jamaica earthquake and coronavirus events analyzed. Limited discussion of methodology limitations.

---

## Conclusion

Applied social media research with appropriate formalism level. Shannon entropy is standard for information analysis and necessary here. Practical contribution through filtering rules for crisis monitoring.

paper.title: An autonomous UAV system for video monitoring of the quarantine zones
paper.abstract: Autonomous UAV system for human detection in quarantine zones. Comparison between SVM with HOG features and deep learning (AlexNet, SqueezeNet, ResNet-18, ResNet-34). Implemented on Raspberry Pi and Jetson Nano companion computers. HoverGames quadcopter with PX4 autopilot.

verdict.summary: Strong applied ML/robotics paper with working hardware implementation. Classification metrics (Eq. 1-5) are standard and necessary for evaluation. Code available on GitHub. Transfer learning comparison across four deep learning architectures.

papers.provenance:

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** UAV-based human detection for quarantine monitoring
**Level:** 2-3 (Applied ML + robotics integration)
**Rationale:** Applies existing ML methods (HOG+SVM, transfer learning) to novel application. Hardware integration provides engineering contribution.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 5 (accuracy, TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV - standard ML metrics)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~10
Expected range (Level 2-3): 5-25
Ratio: 0.4-0.67x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - uses only standard ML evaluation metrics.

### Necessity Test
- Accuracy (Eq. 1): Standard, necessary for performance evaluation
- TPR/TNR/PPV/NPV (Eq. 2-5): Standard metrics, necessary for classification assessment

**Verdict:** All formalism is standard ML evaluation, necessary for comparing methods.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

No self-citation detected. References cover UAV systems, human detection, and deep learning.

---

## Structural Analysis

Well-structured with clear hardware and software descriptions. Code available on GitHub (noted in paper). Comprehensive comparison of classical (HOG+SVM) vs. deep learning approaches. Practical timing results on embedded hardware.

---

## Conclusion

Strong applied research with working prototype. The paper demonstrates practical engineering contribution with rigorous ML evaluation. GitHub code availability is a significant positive. No formalism theater.

paper.title: A Nonlinear Second-order Hyperbolic PDE-based Photon-limited Medical Microscopy Image Restoration Technique
paper.abstract: Novel PDE-based denoising for photon-limited medical microscopy images. Nonlinear hyperbolic second-order PDE model with boundary conditions. Finite difference-based numerical approximation algorithm. Applied to COVID-19 virus microscope images. Removes Poisson noise while preserving details.

verdict.summary: Mathematical image processing paper where PDE formalism IS the core contribution. Hyperbolic PDE model (Eq. 1), diffusivity function (Eq. 2-3), and finite difference scheme (Eq. 6) are essential and cannot be expressed in plain English. Well-posed mathematical contribution.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Novel PDE-based image restoration for Poisson noise removal
**Level:** 3-4 (Mathematical image processing)
**Rationale:** Proposes new hyperbolic PDE model for image denoising. Mathematical formulation IS the contribution. Not just applying existing methods.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0 numbered definitions
- Theorems: 0 (but claims well-posedness)
- Proofs: 0 (numerical validation instead)
- Equations: ~6 (PDE model, diffusivity function, numerical scheme)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~12
Expected range (Level 3-4): 10-60
Ratio: 0.2-0.4x upper bound (conservative use of formalism)

**Assessment:** Proportionate - formalism is the core contribution for mathematical image processing.

### Necessity Test
- Hyperbolic PDE model (Eq. 1): NECESSARY - this IS the contribution, cannot be expressed in prose
- Diffusivity function (Eq. 2-3): NECESSARY - required for edge-preserving diffusion
- Finite difference scheme (Eq. 6): NECESSARY - required for numerical solution

**Verdict:** All formalism is essential. This is a mathematical paper where equations are the contribution.

### Issues
None identified. Appropriate level of formalism for mathematical image processing.

---

## Citation Analysis

2-3 self-citations out of ~20 references. All self-citations are to prior PDE image processing work by the author - relevant to the topic.

---

## Structural Analysis

Well-structured mathematical paper. PSNR/SNR/MSE metrics for validation. Comparison with other methods (Median, TV-based, Bilateral, NLM). COVID-19 microscopy application provides timely relevance.

---

## Conclusion

This is a legitimate mathematical image processing paper where the PDE formalism IS the contribution. The equations cannot be replaced with prose - they define the mathematical model. Well-suited for the journal. No formalism theater.

paper.title: Cough Sound Recognition in Respiratory Disease Epidemics
paper.abstract: Cough sound recognition method for respiratory disease epidemics. Compares feature extractors (SoundNet, auDeep) trained on different corpora (ESC-50, SHC). Experiments with MLP classifiers. Addresses challenges of audio-based disease detection during COVID-19 pandemic.

verdict.summary: Applied deep learning for cough sound recognition. Uses standard audio feature extraction (SoundNet, auDeep) and MLP classification. No unnecessary formalism - focuses on experimental methodology. Relevant to COVID-19 epidemic context.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** Cough sound recognition using deep learning for epidemic monitoring
**Level:** 2-3 (Applied deep learning for audio)
**Rationale:** Applies existing audio feature extractors (SoundNet, auDeep) with transfer learning. Novel aspect is application to epidemic cough detection.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: ~3 (feature concatenation, MLP structure description)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: ~6
Expected range (Level 2-3): 5-25
Ratio: 0.24-0.4x upper bound

**Assessment:** Proportionate - minimal formalism, focuses on experimental methodology.

### Necessity Test
- Feature extraction descriptions: Necessary for reproducibility
- MLP architecture: Standard description, necessary

**Verdict:** Appropriate formalism level for applied ML paper.

### Issues
None identified.

---

## Citation Analysis

No excessive self-citation. Comprehensive literature review on cough detection methods. References cover audio classification, deep learning, and epidemic response.

---

## Structural Analysis

Well-structured experimental paper. Uses public datasets (ESC-50, IRS17, SHC). Four experiments with clear methodology. Discusses challenges of audio-based disease detection. Limited discussion of COVID-19 specific cough characteristics.

---

## Conclusion

Timely applied research addressing cough detection for epidemic monitoring. Uses established deep learning methods (transfer learning) appropriately. No formalism theater - the paper focuses on experimental methodology rather than mathematical derivations.

paper.title: Diagnosis of Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19) from Chest X-Ray images using modified XceptionNet
paper.abstract: Deep learning model for COVID-19 detection from chest X-ray images. Modified XceptionNet with depth-wise separable convolutions. 41-layer model with 6 convolution and 12 separable convolution layers. 95.8% accuracy on three-class classification (Normal, COVID-19, Viral Pneumonia).

verdict.summary: Applied deep learning for COVID-19 detection from X-rays. Uses standard CNN architecture (modified XceptionNet) with depth-wise separable convolutions. Evaluation metrics are standard ML measures. Good comparative study with existing methods.

papers.provenance:

**Topic:** COVID-19 detection from chest X-rays using modified XceptionNet
**Level:** 2 (Applied deep learning)

### Formal Elements

None identified. Appropriate formalism for applied deep learning paper.

---

---

## Conclusion

Solid applied deep learning paper for COVID-19 detection. Uses appropriate formalism for ML evaluation. The paper focuses on practical results rather than theoretical contributions, which is appropriate for the contribution type.

paper.title: Deep Transfer Learning with Apache Spark to Detect COVID-19 in Chest X-ray Images
paper.abstract: Deep Transfer Learning method using CNN models (InceptionV3, ResNet50) with Apache Spark framework for COVID-19 classification in chest X-ray images. 99.01% accuracy with InceptionV3, 98.03% with ResNet50. Implementation in Databricks workspace.

verdict.summary: Applied transfer learning paper combining deep learning with Apache Spark. Uses pre-trained models (InceptionV3, ResNet50) with logistic regression. Minimal formalism - focuses on implementation methodology. Detailed Apache Spark configuration provided.

papers.provenance:
## Formalism Analysis

### Concept Complexity
**Topic:** COVID-19 detection using transfer learning with Apache Spark
**Level:** 2 (Applied transfer learning)
**Rationale:** Applies existing pre-trained models (InceptionV3, ResNet50) with Deep Learning Pipelines on Spark. Engineering contribution rather than algorithmic novelty.

### Formal Elements
- Definitions: 0
- Theorems: 0
- Proofs: 0
- Equations: 0 (only performance metrics reported, no equations stated)

### Proportionality
Calculated complexity: 0
Expected range (Level 2): 0-15
Ratio: 0x (well within range)

**Assessment:** Proportionate - minimal formalism appropriate for implementation paper.

### Necessity Test
No formal elements to evaluate. Paper focuses on implementation methodology and results.

**Verdict:** Appropriate level of formalism for systems/implementation paper.

### Issues
None identified. Paper appropriately focuses on practical implementation.

---

## Citation Analysis

No excessive self-citation. References cover Big Data, Apache Spark, deep learning, and COVID-19 detection.

---

## Structural Analysis

Good implementation details including cluster configuration (Table 2), Spark characteristics (Table 1). Code snippets provided (Fig. 7-8). Uses Databricks workspace. Dataset from Kaggle. Comparison with related work provided.

---

## Conclusion

Practical implementation paper demonstrating Apache Spark for COVID-19 detection. Main contribution is the integration of transfer learning with Big Data framework. Appropriate formalism level for the contribution type. The paper provides detailed implementation guidance which adds practical value.

batch_notes: Volume 23 Special Issue (2020) - COVID-19 and Crisis Management theme. Analyzed with RUIN Framework v2.0 and Necessity Test. **No formalism theater detected.** Paper 649 is Editorial (excluded from analysis). Paper 652 (Vatavu) is explicitly a POSITION PAPER - limited score reflects position paper nature, NOT formalism problems. Remaining 8 research papers all use appropriate formalism: CFD numerical simulation, CA epidemic modeling, PDE image processing, applied ML for COVID-19 detection. Final: 0 CRITICAL, 0 CONCERNING, 0 LIMITED, 8 ADEQUATE, 1 STRONG. Note: The special issue context (rapid COVID-19 response) explains the applied/practical nature of most papers.
